[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=1 f=f5] Epilogue
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=104 b=0 i=0 f=f3] About BB eBooks

[PAGE 7]
[L s=21.2 r=1.42 x=197 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Translator’s Preface
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=163 b=0 i=0 f=f3] A few words about Dostoevsky himself may help the English reader to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=181 b=0 i=0 f=f3] understand his work.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=199 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dostoevsky was the son of a doctor. His parents were very hard-
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=217 b=0 i=0 f=f3] working and deeply religious people, but so poor that they lived with their
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=235 b=0 i=0 f=f3] five children in only two rooms. The father and mother spent their evenings
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=253 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in reading aloud to their children, generally from books of a serious
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=271 b=0 i=0 f=f3] character.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=289 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Though always sickly and delicate Dostoevsky came out third in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=307 b=0 i=0 f=f3] final examination of the Petersburg school of Engineering. There he had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=325 b=0 i=0 f=f3] already begun his first work, “Poor Folk.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=343 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This story was published by the poet Nekrassov in his review and was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=361 b=0 i=0 f=f3] received with acclamations. The shy, unknown youth found himself
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=379 b=0 i=0 f=f3] instantly something of a celebrity. A brilliant and successful career seemed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=397 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to open before him, but those hopes were soon dashed. In 1849 he was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=415 b=0 i=0 f=f3] arrested.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=433 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Though neither by temperament nor conviction a revolutionist,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=451 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dostoevsky was one of a little group of young men who met together to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=469 b=0 i=0 f=f3] read Fourier and Proudhon. He was accused of “taking part in conversations
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=487 b=0 i=0 f=f3] against the censorship, of reading a letter from Byelinsky to Gogol, and of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=505 b=0 i=0 f=f3] knowing of the intention to set up a printing press.” Under Nicholas I. (that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=523 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “stern and just man,” as Maurice Baring calls him) this was enough, and he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=541 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was condemned to death. After eight months’ imprisonment he was with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=559 b=0 i=0 f=f3] twenty-one others taken out to the Semyonovsky Square to be shot. Writing
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=577 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to his brother Mihail, Dostoevsky says: “They snapped words over our
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=595 b=0 i=0 f=f3] heads, and they made us put on the white shirts worn by persons
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=613 b=0 i=0 f=f3] condemned to death. Thereupon we were bound in threes to stakes, to suffer
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=631 b=0 i=0 f=f3] execution. Being the third in the row, I concluded I had only a few minutes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=649 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of life before me. I thought of you and your dear ones and I contrived to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=667 b=0 i=0 f=f3] kiss Plestcheiev and Dourov, who were next to me, and to bid them
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=685 b=0 i=0 f=f3] farewell. Suddenly the troops beat a tattoo, we were unbound, brought back

[PAGE 8]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] upon the scaffold, and informed that his Majesty had spared us our lives.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The sentence was commuted to hard labour.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] One of the prisoners, Grigoryev, went mad as soon as he was untied,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and never regained his sanity.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The intense suffering of this experience left a lasting stamp on
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dostoevsky’s mind. Though his religious temper led him in the end to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accept every suffering with resignation and to regard it as a blessing in his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] own case, he constantly recurs to the subject in his writings. He describes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the awful agony of the condemned man and insists on the cruelty of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inflicting such torture. Then followed four years of penal servitude, spent in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the company of common criminals in Siberia, where he began the “Dead
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] House,” and some years of service in a disciplinary battalion.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had shown signs of some obscure nervous disease before his arrest
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and this now developed into violent attacks of epilepsy, from which he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] suffered for the rest of his life. The fits occurred three or four times a year
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and were more frequent in periods of great strain. In 1859 he was allowed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to return to Russia. He started a journal—“Vremya,” which was forbidden
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] by the Censorship through a misunderstanding. In 1864 he lost his first wife
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and his brother Mihail. He was in terrible poverty, yet he took upon himself
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the payment of his brother’s debts. He started another journal—“The
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Epoch,” which within a few months was also prohibited. He was weighed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] down by debt, his brother’s family was dependent on him, he was forced to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] write at heart-breaking speed, and is said never to have corrected his work.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The later years of his life were much softened by the tenderness and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] devotion of his second wife.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] In June 1880 he made his famous speech at the unveiling of the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] monument to Pushkin in Moscow and he was received with extraordinary
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] demonstrations of love and honour.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] A few months later Dostoevsky died. He was followed to the grave by a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] vast multitude of mourners, who “gave the hapless man the funeral of a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] king.” He is still probably the most widely read writer in Russia.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] In the words of a Russian critic, who seeks to explain the feeling
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inspired by Dostoevsky: “He was one of ourselves, a man of our blood and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] our bone, but one who has suffered and has seen so much more deeply than
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] we have his insight impresses us as wisdom… that wisdom of the heart
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which we seek that we may learn from it how to live. All his other gifts

[PAGE 9]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] came to him from nature, this he won for himself and through it he became
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] great.”

[PAGE 10]
[L s=27.5 r=1.00 x=267 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Part I

[PAGE 11]
[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=223 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter One
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] O
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=297 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] early in July a young man came out of
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=97 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] N AN EXCEPTIONALLY HOT EVENING
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the garret in which he lodged in S. Place and walked slowly, as though in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hesitation, towards K. bridge.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had successfully avoided meeting his landlady on the staircase. His
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] garret was under the roof of a high, five-storied house and was more like a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cupboard than a room. The landlady who provided him with garret, dinners,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and attendance, lived on the floor below, and every time he went out he was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] obliged to pass her kitchen, the door of which invariably stood open. And
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] each time he passed, the young man had a sick, frightened feeling, which
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] made him scowl and feel ashamed. He was hopelessly in debt to his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] landlady, and was afraid of meeting her.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This was not because he was cowardly and abject, quite the contrary;
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] but for some time past he had been in an overstrained irritable condition,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] verging on hypochondria. He had become so completely absorbed in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] himself, and isolated from his fellows that he dreaded meeting, not only his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] landlady, but anyone at all. He was crushed by poverty, but the anxieties of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his position had of late ceased to weigh upon him. He had given up
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] attending to matters of practical importance; he had lost all desire to do so.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Nothing that any landlady could do had a real terror for him. But to be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] stopped on the stairs, to be forced to listen to her trivial, irrelevant gossip, to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pestering demands for payment, threats and complaints, and to rack his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] brains for excuses, to prevaricate, to lie—no, rather than that, he would
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] creep down the stairs like a cat and slip out unseen.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=597 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This evening, however, on coming out into the street, he became acutely
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=615 b=0 i=0 f=f3] aware of his fears.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=633 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I want to attempt a thing like that and am frightened by these trifles,”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he thought, with an odd smile. “Hm… yes, all is in a man’s hands and he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=669 b=0 i=0 f=f3] lets it all slip from cowardice, that’s an axiom. It would be interesting to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=687 b=0 i=0 f=f3] know what it is men are most afraid of. Taking a new step, uttering a new

[PAGE 12]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] word is what they fear most…. But I am talking too much. It’s because I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] chatter that I do nothing. Or perhaps it is that I chatter because I do nothing.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] I’ve learned to chatter this last month, lying for days together in my den
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] thinking… of Jack the Giant-killer. Why am I going there now? Am I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] capable of that? Is that serious? It is not serious at all. It’s simply a fantasy
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to amuse myself; a plaything! Yes, maybe it is a plaything.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The heat in the street was terrible: and the airlessness, the bustle and the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] plaster, scaffolding, bricks, and dust all about him, and that special
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petersburg stench, so familiar to all who are unable to get out of town in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] summer—all worked painfully upon the young man’s already overwrought
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] nerves. The insufferable stench from the pot-houses, which are particularly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] numerous in that part of the town, and the drunken men whom he met
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] continually, although it was a working day, completed the revolting misery
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of the picture. An expression of the profoundest disgust gleamed for a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] moment in the young man’s refined face. He was, by the way, exceptionally
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] handsome, above the average in height, slim, well-built, with beautiful dark
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] eyes and dark brown hair. Soon he sank into deep thought, or more
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accurately speaking into a complete blankness of mind; he walked along not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] observing what was about him and not caring to observe it. From time to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] time, he would mutter something, from the habit of talking to himself, to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which he had just confessed. At these moments he would become conscious
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that his ideas were sometimes in a tangle and that he was very weak; for
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] two days he had scarcely tasted food.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He was so badly dressed that even a man accustomed to shabbiness
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would have been ashamed to be seen in the street in such rags. In that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] quarter of the town, however, scarcely any shortcoming in dress would have
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] created surprise. Owing to the proximity of the Hay Market, the number of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] establishments of bad character, the preponderance of the trading and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] working class population crowded in these streets and alleys in the heart of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petersburg, types so various were to be seen in the streets that no figure,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] however queer, would have caused surprise. But there was such
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accumulated bitterness and contempt in the young man’s heart, that, in spite
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of all the fastidiousness of youth, he minded his rags least of all in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] street. It was a different matter when he met with acquaintances or with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] former fellow students, whom, indeed, he disliked meeting at any time. And
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] yet when a drunken man who, for some unknown reason, was being taken

[PAGE 13]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] somewhere in a huge waggon dragged by a heavy dray horse, suddenly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] shouted at him as he drove past: “Hey there, German hatter” bawling at the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] top of his voice and pointing at him—the young man stopped suddenly and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] clutched tremulously at his hat. It was a tall round hat from Zimmerman’s,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] but completely worn out, rusty with age, all torn and bespattered, brimless
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and bent on one side in a most unseemly fashion. Not shame, however, but
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] quite another feeling akin to terror had overtaken him.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I knew it,” he muttered in confusion, “I thought so! That’s the worst of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] all! Why, a stupid thing like this, the most trivial detail might spoil the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] whole plan. Yes, my hat is too noticeable…. It looks absurd and that makes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] it noticeable…. With my rags I ought to wear a cap, any sort of old
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pancake, but not this grotesque thing. Nobody wears such a hat, it would be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] noticed a mile off, it would be remembered…. What matters is that people
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would remember it, and that would give them a clue. For this business one
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] should be as little conspicuous as possible…. Trifles, trifles are what
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] matter! Why, it’s just such trifles that always ruin everything….”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had not far to go; he knew indeed how many steps it was from the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] gate of his lodging house: exactly seven hundred and thirty. He had counted
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] them once when he had been lost in dreams. At the time he had put no faith
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in those dreams and was only tantalising himself by their hideous but daring
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] recklessness. Now, a month later, he had begun to look upon them
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] differently, and, in spite of the monologues in which he jeered at his own
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] impotence and indecision, he had involuntarily come to regard this
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “hideous” dream as an exploit to be attempted, although he still did not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] realise this himself. He was positively going now for a “rehearsal” of his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] project, and at every step his excitement grew more and more violent.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] With a sinking heart and a nervous tremor, he went up to a huge house
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which on one side looked on to the canal, and on the other into the street.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This house was let out in tiny tenements and was inhabited by working
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] people of all kinds—tailors, locksmiths, cooks, Germans of sorts, girls
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] picking up a living as best they could, petty clerks, etc. There was a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] continual coming and going through the two gates and in the two courtyards
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of the house. Three or four door-keepers were employed on the building.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The young man was very glad to meet none of them, and at once slipped
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unnoticed through the door on the right, and up the staircase. It was a back
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] staircase, dark and narrow, but he was familiar with it already, and knew his
